steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: "Zitkála-Šá was the cofounder of the National Council of American ______ advocated for Native American civil rights throughout the 1920s and 1930s." The blank must connect "National Council of American Indians" to a verb phrase ("advocated for...") that needs a subject. This requires a relative clause introduced by a relative pronoun.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Indians. Which': Creates a sentence fragment — "Which advocated for Native American civil rights" cannot stand alone as a sentence. A relative pronoun cannot begin a new sentence. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Indians,': A comma alone without a relative pronoun leaves "advocated for Native American civil rights" without a subject, producing a comma splice or dangling verb phrase. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Indians': No punctuation and no relative pronoun makes "advocated" a second main verb with no subject, creating a run-on. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Indians, which': A comma followed by the relative pronoun "which" correctly introduces a non-restrictive relative clause modifying "National Council of American Indians," giving "advocated" the subject it needs. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
